title: Same-Same
narrative:
Nicolas owns a small pub on the outskirts of London. He loves to take long walks, watch sunsets aaaaand hustle tourists in cards who come to his pub. His favourite game is called "Same-Same".

To play Same-Same, you need a standard pack of 52 cards. Each card has a suit (Diamonds — D, Clubs — C, Spades — S, or Hearts — H), and a rank (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, T, J, Q, K, or A).

At the start of the game, there is one card on the table and you have five cards in your hand. You can play a card from your hand if and only if it has the same rank or the same suit as the card on the table.

In order to check if you'd be a good person to try to hustle, Nicolas has prepared a test for you. Given the card on the table and five cards in your hand, check if you can play at least one card.
input_spec:
x is a 2-element list:
  x[0]: a string — the card on the table (e.g. "AS" = Ace of Spades)
  x[1]: a list of 5 strings — the cards in your hand

Each card string is 2 characters: rank in {2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,T,J,Q,K,A} and suit in {D,C,S,H}.
All cards in the input are different.
output_spec: Return the string "YES" if you can play at least one card, otherwise "NO".

examples.note: AS on table. AD in hand shares rank (Ace). Play it.
Nothing shares rank 2 or suit H.
KS shares rank K with KH.
Nothing shares rank 9 or suit H.

hints: Cards are just strings — x[0] is a 2-char string like "AS". x[0][0] is the rank char 'A', x[0][1] is the suit char 'S'.
x[1] is a list of 5 strings. To get all ranks: x[1][;0]. To get all suits: x[1][;1]. This is matrix indexing — try it in q!
Use `any` to check if any element of a boolean list is true.
Use `in` or `=` to compare a char against a list of chars.
Use `\t` to measure how fast your function is: `\t:1000 func each x`
winning_criteria: FASTEST correct single-parameter func wins. If within 10% of the fastest timing, shortest code wins. Measured with: `\t:1000 func each x` and `-2+count string func`.
editorial:
## Approach

Given a table card `x[0]` and a hand `x[1]` (5 cards), you need to check if any card shares a rank **or** suit.

### Key q insight: matrix indexing

In q, a list of 2-char strings is a matrix. You can extract a whole column in one shot:

```q
x[1][;0]  / all rank chars, e.g. "ATJK2"
x[1][;1]  / all suit chars, e.g. "HCSDH"
```

### Step-by-step

1. Extract the table rank: `x[0;0]` and table suit: `x[0;1]`
2. Check `x[0;0] in x[1][;0]` — is the rank in any hand card?
3. Check `x[0;1] in x[1][;1]` — is the suit in any hand card?
4. Return `"YES"` if either is true, else `"NO"`

```q
func:{$[x[0;0]in x[1;;0];"YES";x[0;1]in x[1;;1];"YES";"NO"]}
```

`$[cond;true;cond2;true2;false]` is q's multi-way conditional — it short-circuits on the first true condition.

### Optimising further

Can you express both checks as a single `any` over a combined boolean list? Try `any x[0]in'flip x[1]`.
